The *mass number* of an atom is the sum of its protons and neutrons. Atoms of elements will always have the same atomic number (i.e. number of protons), but in some cases they may have different numbers of neutrons and hence different mass numbers (see Figure 3.2 for examples). Such atoms are referred to as *isotopes*. In most cases, these are *stable isotopes* because their nuclear structure does not change over time. Atoms of the common elements of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, for example, can exist as stable isotopes. Although isotopes of an element have different numbers of neutrons, they each have identical chemical properties because they all contain the same number of electrons. Progressing from what you learned from Figure 3.1, Figure 3.2 shows how the atomic structure of specific atoms differ from one another on the basis of the number of protons they have.

### 3.1.4 Atomic Mass

## Definition

The mass number of an atom is the sum of its protons and neutrons. Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same atomic number / number of protons) that differ in the number of neutrons, and therefore in mass number.

## Core Principles

- The atomic number of an element is fixed by its number of protons; all atoms of a given element share the same atomic number.
- Variation in neutron number among atoms of the same element produces different mass numbers, giving rise to isotopes.
- Most naturally occurring isotopes of common biological elements (e.g. carbon, nitrogen, oxygen) are *stable isotopes*, meaning their nuclear structure does not change over time.
- Despite differing neutron counts, isotopes of an element exhibit identical chemical properties because chemical behaviour is determined by the number of electrons, which is equal across isotopes of the same element.

## Sub-concepts

- **Mass number**: total count of protons + neutrons in the nucleus.
- **Atomic number**: number of protons; defines elemental identity.
- **Isotope**: atom of an element with a neutron count (and hence mass number) different from another atom of the same element.
- **Stable isotope**: isotope whose nucleus does not undergo decay over time; carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen all have stable isotopic forms.

## Field-level Controversies

## 文獻分歧 / Discussion

## Practical Applications

- Stable isotopes of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen are used as non-radioactive tracers in biochemistry and exercise physiology research, exploiting the fact that their chemical behaviour matches the more abundant isotope while their mass difference allows detection.
